questions.stem: A nurse is monitoring a patient on telemetry when the rhythm suddenly changes to a wide-complex, regular tachycardia at 180 bpm. The patient is alert, with BP 102/60 mmHg and reports palpitations but no chest pain. Which intervention should the nurse anticipate?

options.text: Immediate unsynchronized defibrillation at 200J
Prepare for synchronized cardioversion at 100J after sedation
Administer IV adenosine 6 mg rapid push to terminate the rhythm
Apply a 12-lead ECG, notify the provider, and prepare antiarrhythmic therapy (amiodarone or procainamide) while monitoring hemodynamics closely

questions.rationale: Wide-complex tachycardia (WCT) in a hemodynamically stable patient (alert, BP 102/60, no chest pain) requires accurate rhythm identification before treatment. WCT is most commonly ventricular tachycardia (VT) but can also be supraventricular tachycardia with aberrant conduction (SVT-A). A 12-lead ECG helps differentiate. If VT is confirmed: amiodarone or procainamide IV is the antiarrhythmic of choice for stable VT. Synchronized cardioversion is reserved for hemodynamically unstable stable VT (BP < 90, ischemia, altered mental status). Unsynchronized defibrillation (defib) is for pulseless VT/VF. Adenosine is appropriate for narrow-complex SVT; it is not first-line for wide-complex tachycardia and can be harmful in VT.

distractorRationales.a: Immediate unsynchronized defibrillation at 200J would not address the priority, and misses that Wide-complex tachycardia (WCT) in a hemodynamically stable patient (alert, BP 102/60, no chest pain) requires accurate rhythm identification. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Apply a 12-lead ECG, notify the provider, and prepare antiarrhythmic therapy (amiodarone or procainamide) while monitoring, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.b: This answer choice treats Prepare for synchronized cardioversion at 100J after sedation as the key issue, despite the clinical warning in Stable wide-complex tachycardia: 12-lead first → provider → antiarrhythmic (amiodarone or procainamide for VT). Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Apply a 12-lead ECG, notify the provider, and prepare antiarrhythmic therapy (amiodarone or procainamide) while monitoring; otherwise circulation or perfusion remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: This answer choice treats Administer IV adenosine 6 mg rapid push to terminate the rhythm as the key issue, while the decisive cue is Stable wide-complex tachycardia: 12-lead first → provider → antiarrhythmic (amiodarone or procainamide for VT). Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay circulation and perfusion support and leave circulation or perfusion unresolved.

questions.takeaway: Stable wide-complex tachycardia: 12-lead first → provider → antiarrhythmic (amiodarone or procainamide for VT). Unstable VT = synchronized cardioversion. Pulseless VT = unsynchronized defibrillation. Never defibrillate a patient who has a pulse.

visualRationale.title: Cardiovascular / Dysrhythmias
visualRationale.caption: Stable wide-complex tachycardia: 12-lead first → provider → antiarrhythmic (amiodarone or procainamide for VT). Unstable VT = synchronized cardioversion. Pulseless VT = unsynchronized defibrillation. Never defibrillate a patient who has a pulse.
visualRationale.conclusion: Stable wide-complex tachycardia: 12-lead first → provider → antiarrhythmic (amiodarone or procainamide for VT). Unstable VT = synchronized cardioversion. Pulseless VT = unsynchronized defibrillation. Never defibrillate a patient who has a pulse.

questions.stem: A nurse is assessing vital signs in the pediatric unit. Which set of vital signs is within normal limits for a healthy 3-year-old toddler?
questions.rationale: Normal vital signs for a 3-year-old toddler: HR 80–130 bpm, RR 22–37 breaths/min, BP approximately 90–100/55–65 mmHg. HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56 are all within expected ranges. Choice A has a bradycardic HR and adult-level BP. Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress, and RR 40 is elevated. Choice D has significant bradycardia. Key developmental milestone: vital signs normalize toward adult ranges with age — neonates have the highest HR and RR, lowest BP.

distractorRationales.a: The reasoning trap in HR 68, RR 14, BP 120/80 is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, and misses that RR 40 is elevated. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for airway and breathing intervention while oxygenation or ventilation progresses.
distractorRationales.c: The clinical error in HR 145, RR 40, BP 80/48 is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, and misses that RR 40 is elevated. The correct path is HR 100, RR 26, BP 92/56, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on HR 55, RR 18, BP 110/72, although the nurse should be guided by Choice C has tachycardia that could suggest distress. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.

questions.takeaway: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

visualRationale.title: Pediatric Nursing
visualRationale.caption: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.
visualRationale.conclusion: Pediatric VS by age: newborn HR 120–160, RR 30–60; toddler HR 80–130, RR 22–37; school-age HR 70–110, RR 18–25; adolescent approaches adult norms.

questions.stem: A patient is scheduled for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy at 8:00 AM. Per current ASA NPO guidelines, what are the correct restrictions for solid food and clear liquids?

options.text: Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid
Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery
Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, clear juice, black coffee) allowed until 2 hours before
No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk

questions.rationale: Current ASA NPO guidelines (2017, updated 2023): Solid foods, fried/fatty meals: NPO ≥8 hours. Light meals (toast, clear liquids): NPO ≥6 hours. Non-clear liquids including milk and formula: NPO ≥6 hours. Clear liquids (water, apple juice without pulp, clear carbonated beverages, black coffee or tea without cream, sports drinks): may be consumed up to 2 hours before elective procedures. Chewing gum and hard candy: treat as clear liquid — stop 2 hours before. These evidence-based guidelines replaced the outdated 'NPO after midnight' rule. The old rule increased patient discomfort, insulin resistance, and perioperative complications without reducing aspiration risk for clear fluids. High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction.

distractorRationales.a: This option makes Nothing by mouth after midnight — no exceptions for any type of food or liquid the focus, even though the higher-risk finding is High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water,, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.b: This option makes Solid food stopped by midnight; clear liquids (including milk) allowed until 4 hours before surgery the focus, when the bedside priority is driven by High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water, is safer because it addresses unstable glucose physiology before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: No restrictions apply — laparoscopic procedures do not carry aspiration risk would not address the priority, and misses that High-risk patients (morbid obesity, GERD, pregnancy, gastroparesis, bowel obstruction) require individualized assessment and may need longer restriction. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Solid food (including non-clear liquids and milk) stopped at least 6-8 hours before; clear liquids (water,, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: ASA NPO: solids ≥6-8 hrs before; clear liquids until 2 hrs before. 'NPO after midnight' is outdated and harmful. Clear liquids: water, clear juice (no pulp), black coffee/tea.

questions.stem: A nurse is preparing to administer insulin glargine (Lantus) and insulin aspart (NovoLog) to a patient with type 1 diabetes before breakfast. Which action by the nurse requires IMMEDIATE correction?

options.text: Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe
Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration
Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal
Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh

questions.rationale: Insulin glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting basal insulin with a specific pH formulation. It should NEVER be mixed with any other insulin, including rapid-acting insulins. Mixing glargine with other insulins alters its pH, causing it to crystallize and unpredictably changes both insulins' absorption profiles. The result is an unreliable pharmacokinetic profile that can cause severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia. Each insulin must be drawn in a separate syringe and administered as separate injections. Drawing up separately is correct. Injecting aspart before meals (pre-prandial) is appropriate. Site rotation is correct practice.

distractorRationales.a: The problem with Drawing up each insulin in a separate syringe is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, rather than acting on It has its own pH and formulation. That mismatch risks slower recognition of unstable glucose physiology and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: Injecting the aspart subcutaneously 15 minutes before the meal would not address the priority, even though the higher-risk finding is Insulin glargine (Lantus) is a long-acting basal insulin with a specific pH formulation. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration is safer because it addresses unstable glucose physiology before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on Rotating injection sites between the abdomen and thigh, instead of responding to The result is an unreliable pharmacokinetic profile that can cause severe hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The safer answer is Mixing glargine and aspart in the same syringe before administration because delaying glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization can worsen unstable glucose physiology.

questions.takeaway: Glargine (Lantus) = NEVER MIX with other insulins. It has its own pH and formulation. Mix-up can cause crystal formation and unpredictable glycemic effect. Use separate syringes, separate injections. Applies also to detemir (Levemir).

questions.stem: A nurse is caring for four patients on a medical-surgical unit. Which patient should the nurse assess first?

options.text: A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL
A 44-year-old post-appendectomy (day 2) requesting pain medication, pain rated 5/10
A 71-year-old with heart failure who gained 1 lb overnight and is on daily weights
A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due

questions.rationale: The patient with a blood glucose of 62 mg/dL has symptomatic hypoglycemia ('shaky'), which is an acute, life-threatening condition requiring immediate intervention. Untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness, and brain injury. The other patients represent important but lower-acuity concerns: the post-op patient has manageable pain, the heart failure patient's weight gain needs assessment but is not immediately life-threatening, and the UTI patient's antibiotic timing is a scheduled task.

distractorRationales.b: A 44-year-old post-appendectomy (day 2) requesting pain medication, pain rated 5/10 would not address the priority, without accounting for Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The clinical error in A 71-year-old with heart failure who gained 1 lb overnight and is on daily weights is that it anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, when the bedside priority is driven by Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine. A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of is safer because it addresses unstable glucose physiology before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.d: This option makes A 33-year-old with a UTI who asks when the next dose of antibiotics is due the focus, when the bedside priority is driven by Untreated hypoglycemia can rapidly progress to seizures, loss of consciousness. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so It is less defensible because A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes who reports feeling 'shaky' and has a blood glucose of targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Apply the ABCs and Maslow's hierarchy: acute metabolic emergencies (hypoglycemia, airway compromise) always outrank scheduled pain requests, routine monitoring, and informational questions.

questions.stem: A patient with septic shock is intubated and mechanically ventilated on FiO2 0.65. ABG results: pH 7.28, PaCO2 32 mmHg, HCO3 14 mEq/L, PaO2 74 mmHg. The provider asks the nurse to identify the acid-base disturbance and whether compensation is adequate. Which interpretation is CORRECT?

options.text: Respiratory alkalosis with metabolic compensation; adequate
Metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation; compensation is incomplete
Mixed metabolic and respiratory acidosis; no compensation present
Metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation; compensation is excessive

questions.rationale: Step-by-step: (1) pH 7.28 = acidosis. (2) HCO3 14 = metabolic acidosis (primary). (3) PaCO2 32 = low, meaning lungs are compensating by blowing off CO2 (respiratory compensation). (4) Winter's formula checks adequacy: expected PaCO2 = 1.5(HCO3) + 8 ±2 = 1.5(14)+8 = 29 ±2 = 27–31. Actual PaCO2 is 32, which is slightly above the expected range, meaning compensation is present but slightly incomplete. This pattern is consistent with metabolic acidosis from septic lactic acidosis with partial respiratory compensation.

distractorRationales.a: This answer choice treats Respiratory alkalosis with metabolic compensation; adequate as the key issue, despite the clinical warning in (2) HCO3 14 = metabolic acidosis (primary). Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The safer answer is Metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation; compensation is incomplete because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.
distractorRationales.c: This option overweights Mixed metabolic and respiratory acidosis; no compensation present, although the nurse should be guided by 5×HCO3 + 8 ±2) to check adequacy of respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: This answer choice treats Metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation; compensation is excessive as the key issue, despite the clinical warning in (2) HCO3 14 = metabolic acidosis (primary). Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so The safer answer is Metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation; compensation is incomplete because delaying airway and breathing intervention can worsen oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Use Winter's formula (1.5×HCO3 + 8 ±2) to check adequacy of respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis.

questions.stem: A patient who has been on chlorpromazine for 10 years develops involuntary, repetitive tongue protrusions, lip-smacking movements, and facial grimacing that persist even when the medication is held. Which condition does this describe?

options.text: Acute dystonia
Tardive dyskinesia
Akathisia
Pseudoparkinsonism

questions.rationale: Tardive dyskinesia (TD) develops after months to years of antipsychotic use. It is characterized by involuntary, repetitive, purposeless movements of the face, tongue, and extremities (lip smacking, tongue protrusion, choreiform movements). Key feature: movements PERSIST or worsen when the antipsychotic is reduced/stopped initially. It may be irreversible. VMAT2 inhibitors (valbenazine, deutetrabenazine) are FDA-approved treatments. Acute dystonia (A) is sudden onset within days and responds to anticholinergics. Akathisia (C) is restlessness and urge to move. Pseudoparkinsonism (D) mimics Parkinson's disease.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor pulls attention toward Acute dystonia, but the stem cue is A patient who has been on chlorpromazine for 10 years develops involuntary, repetitive tongue protrusions, lip-smacking movements. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Tardive dyskinesia, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.c: Akathisia would not address the priority, but the stem cue is A patient who has been on chlorpromazine for 10 years develops involuntary, repetitive tongue protrusions, lip-smacking movements. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so It is less defensible because Tardive dyskinesia targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: This option makes Pseudoparkinsonism the focus, even though the higher-risk finding is Acute dystonia (A) is sudden onset within days and responds to anticholinergics. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Tardive dyskinesia is safer because it addresses medication toxicity before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Tardive dyskinesia: involuntary repetitive orofacial movements after years of antipsychotic use — may be irreversible; treat with VMAT2 inhibitors.

questions.stem: A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention. Which example correctly illustrates primary prevention?

options.text: Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40
Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season
Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture
Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps

questions.rationale: Primary prevention: actions taken before disease occurs to prevent onset. Examples: immunization, health education, smoking cessation counseling, fluoridated water, condom use. Secondary prevention: early detection of existing disease (screening) — mammography, colonoscopy, blood pressure screening, Pap smear. Tertiary prevention: reducing disability and restoring function in patients with established disease — rehabilitation, cardiac rehab, chronic disease management. Mammography and colonoscopy are secondary prevention (detecting disease before symptoms). Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention.

distractorRationales.a: This distractor pulls attention toward Providing mammography screening to detect early breast cancer in women over 40, but the stem cue is Physical therapy post-fracture is tertiary prevention. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season is safer because it addresses circulation or perfusion before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: The reasoning trap in Prescribing physical therapy for a patient recovering from a hip fracture is that it anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, even though the higher-risk finding is Tertiary prevention: reducing disability and restoring function in patients with established disease — rehabilitation, cardiac rehab, chronic disease. The correct path is Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: This choice focuses on Conducting a colonoscopy to identify and remove pre-cancerous polyps, rather than acting on A community health nurse is teaching a group of nursing students about levels of prevention. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is Administering the influenza vaccine to a healthy adult before flu season, not a step that delays stabilization of circulation or perfusion.

questions.takeaway: Primary prevention = before disease (vaccines, education); Secondary = early detection (screening); Tertiary = limit disability/restore function (rehab). Not to be confused with clinical levels of care.

questions.stem: A nurse is completing an admission assessment on a 79-year-old patient transferred from a nursing facility. Which combination of factors identifies the HIGHEST fall risk?

options.text: Age >65 and female gender
History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic drop >20 mmHg on standing)
Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29
Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion

questions.rationale: The highest fall risk constellation combines: (1) Prior fall history — the single strongest predictor of future falls in the elderly; (2) Polypharmacy with antihypertensives — alpha-blockers, diuretics, and calcium channel blockers commonly cause orthostatic hypotension and syncope; (3) Documented orthostatic hypotension — directly causes dizziness and falls upon position change. Both the Morse Fall Scale and STRATIFY risk tool weight prior falls most heavily. Key interventions: bed in lowest position, non-skid footwear, call light within reach, scheduled toileting (most falls occur during nighttime toileting), orthostatic vital signs before ambulation, and urgent medication review. Age and gender alone are poor predictors without functional factors.

distractorRationales.a: This option overweights Age >65 and female gender, rather than acting on Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of medication toxicity and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.c: The problem with Mild bilateral hearing loss and BMI of 29 is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, instead of responding to Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. The priority is History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic, not a step that delays stabilization of medication toxicity.
distractorRationales.d: Mild arthritis in both knees and occasional evening confusion fits a lower-risk interpretation, despite the clinical warning in Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so The safer answer is History of fall in the past 6 months, taking 3 antihypertensive medications, and orthostatic hypotension (systolic because delaying medication-safety intervention can worsen medication toxicity.

questions.takeaway: Highest fall risk: prior fall + polypharmacy (antihypertensives) + orthostatic hypotension. Prior fall is the strongest single predictor. Target modifiable risk factors aggressively.

questions.stem: An RN is caring for four patients and needs to delegate tasks. Which task is appropriate to assign to an unlicensed assistive personnel (UAP/CNA)?

options.text: Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea
Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube
Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery
Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care

questions.rationale: UAPs can perform tasks that are routine, repetitive, and do not require nursing judgment or assessment. Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the RN has assessed the patient's stability. Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Administering tube feedings (B) — in many states, enteral feeding via established G-tube can be delegated to trained UAPs, but the skill level required is greater than ambulation; this varies by state — ambulation is universally appropriate for CNA delegation. Patient education (D) is an RN responsibility requiring professional judgment.

distractorRationales.a: Assess the breath sounds of a patient with new-onset dyspnea is not the best answer, when the bedside priority is driven by UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.b: This answer choice treats Administer a tube feeding to a stable patient via an established G-tube as the key issue, although the nurse should be guided by Assessment of breath sounds (A) requires nursing judgment and cannot be delegated. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so The nurse should prioritize Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery; otherwise oxygenation or ventilation remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.d: Teach a diabetic patient how to perform foot care is not the best answer, but the stem cue is Ambulating a stable postoperative patient 24 hours post-hip replacement is a safe, predictable task for UAP after the. Moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, so The correct path is Ambulate a postoperative patient 24 hours after hip replacement surgery, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.

questions.takeaway: UAPs can perform routine, non-assessment tasks: ambulation, ADLs, vital signs on stable patients, I&O — assessment, teaching, and medication administration remain with the RN.
